senses.definition: to lessen the amount of money or resources one uses compared to before, particularly due to having less available

examples.x: With unexpected medical bills, he realized he needed to tighten his belt and start budgeting more carefully.

word_list.pronunciation: stɹˈɛtʃ wˈʌnz lˈɛɡz ɐkˈoːɹdɪŋ tə ðə kˈʌvɚlət

senses.definition: to avoid spending more money than what one earns

examples.x: I knew you want to go on that expense vacation, but you need to stretch your legs according to the coverlet.

word_list.pronunciation: stɹˈɛtʃ wˈʌnz ˈɑːɹm nˈoʊ ˌɛni fˈɜːðɚ ðɐn wˈʌnz slˈiːv wɪl ɹˈiːtʃ

senses.definition: The origin of this idiom is not clear, but it likely developed from the idea of being cautious and not overreaching or attempting more than one can handle.

examples.x: She needs to prioritize her work and stretch her arm no further than her sleeve will reach to avoid burnout.

senses.definition: to continue to waste a lot of money on something that is not worth it

examples.x: I know you've already spent a lot on repairs for that old car, but don't throw good money after bad.
The company is facing severe financial losses, and investing more in this failing project would be like throwing good money after bad.
They keep buying more lottery tickets, thinking they'll win eventually, but it's just throwing good money after bad.

senses.definition: a project, activity, business, etc. that uses a lot of money or resources without providing any results or profit

examples.x: The expansion plan for the business turned into a black hole, draining financial resources without increasing profits.
The government invested heavily in the infrastructure project, but it turned out to be a black hole with little tangible benefit to the public.
The new marketing campaign turned out to be a black hole for the company's budget as it didn't attract any new customers.
The research project consumed enormous funds but resulted in no significant discoveries, making it a black hole of resources.

senses.definition: to possess a significant amount of wealth but lack good judgment or wisdom in managing it
to spend in a way that exceeds one's income

examples.x: Despite having a modest salary, he tried to maintain a lavish lifestyle, living beyond his means and neglecting to save for the future.
She often used credit cards for unnecessary shopping, which resulted in living beyond her means and accumulating debt.
The couple took frequent extravagant vacations, leading to significant debt as they were living beyond their means.

senses.definition: to spend no more money than one has

examples.x: Despite having a high salary, she chose to live within her means, avoiding unnecessary expenses and building up savings.
He was content with his modest lifestyle, always living within his means and avoiding extravagant purchases.
The couple prioritized living within their means, choosing to invest and save for their future rather than indulge in excessive spending.
The family made a budget to ensure they could live within their means and save for their children's education.
They decided to downsize their lifestyle and live within their means to eliminate credit card debt and achieve financial stability.

word_list.pronunciation: mˈʌni bˈɜːn ɐ hˈoʊl ɪn wˈʌnz pˈɑːkɪt

senses.definition: to have a strong desire to spend money quickly or impulsively, often resulting in reckless spending habits

examples.x: After receiving his bonus, he felt like the money was burning a hole in his pocket and immediately went on a shopping spree.
She won a small lottery prize and felt the money was burning a hole in her pocket, prompting her to splurge on luxury items.
The child was so excited about her birthday money that it seemed to burn a hole in her pocket, and she couldn't wait to spend it on toys.

senses.definition: to wastefully and recklessly spend money

examples.x: He felt like he was throwing money out of the window when he realized how much he spent on subscription services he rarely used.
Instead of saving for the future, she was throwing money out of the window on unnecessary luxury items.
The company's poor financial management resulted in throwing money out the window, leading to bankruptcy.
The extravagant wedding seemed to be an event where they were throwing money out of the window without considering practicality.

senses.definition: to carelessly spend a great sum of money

word_list.pronunciation: plˈeɪ dˈʌks ænd dɹˈeɪks wɪð ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to use or handle something in a wasteful and careless manner

examples.x: She played ducks and drakes with her inheritance, squandering it on extravagant purchases.
The company's management seemed to be playing ducks and drakes with their finances, leading to financial instability.
Instead of saving for the future, he played ducks and drakes with his savings, spending it on impulsive vacations.
The careless handling of the sensitive information by the employee showed that he was playing ducks and drakes with the company's data security.
It was clear that the government had been playing ducks and drakes with environmental regulations, leading to ecological damage.

senses.definition: money that is put aside as savings for the future or special occasions

examples.x: She saved diligently to build a substantial nest egg for her retirement.
The company encourages employees to invest in a retirement plan for their nest egg.
They used their bonuses as a nest egg for a down payment on a house.

senses.definition: someone that spends money in an extravagant way

examples.x: Mike lived the life of a high roller, staying at the finest hotels and dining at Michelin-starred restaurants.
She acted like a high roller during her shopping spree, purchasing designer clothes and expensive jewelry.
The billionaire entrepreneur is known as a high roller, frequently buying luxury yachts and private jets.

word_list.pronunciation: kˈʌt wˈʌnz kˈoʊt ɐkˈoːɹdɪŋ tʊ wˈʌnz klˈɔθ

senses.definition: to live in a way that does not exceed one's financial limitations

examples.x: As a responsible business owner, she always cuts her coat according to her cloth, avoiding unnecessary expenses.
The government is forced to cut its coat according to its cloth, as the economy faces challenges.
Tom learned to cut his coat according to his cloth after facing financial difficulties due to overspending.

senses.definition: to have more money than one needs and waste it on unnecessary things

examples.x: After winning the lottery, he had money to burn and started buying luxury cars and expensive jewelry.
The company's CEO earns a substantial salary and has money to burn on lavish vacations.
The millionaire heiress can dine at the most exclusive restaurants; she has money to burn.

senses.definition: used to refer to something on which one keeps spending more and more money

examples.x: The ambitious startup turned into a money pit, with investors pouring funds without seeing any profit.
The old house they bought turned out to be a money pit, as it required constant repairs and renovations.
The vintage car he bought became a money pit, as it needed frequent repairs and costly spare parts.

senses.definition: to try to spend as little money as possible

examples.x: Despite their low income, they managed to scrimp and scrape enough to go on a modest vacation.
In order to afford a new car, she had to scrimp and save for several months.
The young couple scrimp and save every month to build their savings for a down payment on a house.
